Cancer histone mutations impact protein binding and DNA repair with possible links to genomic instability Daniel Espiritu 1 , Yiru Sheng2 , Yunhui Peng 3 , Daria Ostroverkhova1 , Shuxiang Li1 , David Landsman 4 , Maria J. Aristizabal 2 ,5 ,*, Anna R. Panchenko1 ,2 ,6 ,7 ,8 ,* Department of Pathology and Molecular Medicine, Queen’s University, Kingston, ON K7L 2V6, Canada Department of Biomedical and Molecular Sciences, Queen’s University, Kingston, ON K7L 2V7, Canada Institute of Biophysics and Department of Physics, Central China Normal University, Wuhan 430079, China National Library of Medicine, National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD 20894, United States Biology Department, Queen’s University, Kingston, ON K7L 3N6, Canada School of Computing, Queen’s University, Kingston, ON K7L 2N8, Canada Adaptive Oncology, Ontario Institute of Cancer Research, Toronto, ON M5G 1M1, Canada Department of Physics, Engineering Physics & Astronomy, Queen’s University, Kingston, ON K7L 3N6, Canada

To whom correspondence should be addressed. Email: anna.panchenko@queensu.ca Correspondence may also be addressed to Maria J. Aristizabal. Email: maria.aristizabal@queensu.ca

Abstract Histones are key epigenetic factors that regulate the accessibility and compaction of eukaryotic genomes, affecting DNA replication and repair, and gene expression. Recent studies have demonstrated that histone missense mutations can perturb normal histone function, promoting the development of phenotypically distinguishable cancers. However, most histone mutations observed in cancer patients remain enigmatic in their potential to promote cancer development. To assess the oncogenic potential of histone missense mutations, we have gathered wholeexome sequencing data for the tumors of about 12 000 patients. Histone mutations occurred in about 16% of cancer patients, although specific cancer types showed substantially higher rates. Using genomic, structural, and biophysical analyses, we found several predominant modes of action by which histone mutations may alter function. Namely, cancer missense mutations primarily affected histone acidic patch residues and protein-binding interfaces in a cancer-specific manner and targeted interaction interfaces with specific DNA repair proteins. Consistent with this finding, we observed a high tumor mutational burden in patients with histone mutations affecting interactions with proteins involved in maintaining genome integrity. We identified potential cancer driver mutations in several histone genes, including mutations on histone H4—a highly conserved histone without previously documented driver mutations.

Introduction

Materials and methods Identification of somatic histone mutations Genetic and clinical data were gathered from patients in The Cancer Genome Atlas, Multiple Myeloma Research Foundation CoMMpass, Human Cancer Model Initiative, Count Me In, and Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium programs through the Genomic Data Commons (GDC) [31]. Patients with benign neoplasms were identified based on their primary diagnosis and removed from the dataset. Primary diagnoses follow morphological diagnosis guidelines from the

Histones are highly conserved nuclear proteins that are found ubiquitously in eukaryotic cells. They are key elements of nucleosomes, which consist of ∼147 base pairs of DNA, two copies each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4 histones, and ∼20–25 base pairs of linker DNA [1, 2]. H1 histones can bind linker DNA, forming chromatosomes. Nucleosomes and chromatosomes represent the fundamental subunits of chromatin. Overall, nucleosomes and chromatosomes are essential components of DNA packaging, act as hub points in epigenetic regulatory pathways, modulate DNA accessibility, and regulate DNA replication, repair, and transcription [1]. Chemical moieties (post-translational modifications, PTMs) can be added or removed from histone proteins, altering interactions with DNA and nucleosome-binding proteins [3]. Although histones are evolutionarily conserved and critical for upholding genomic integrity, histone genes are prone to accumulating mutations [4, 5]. Some missense mutations result in mutant histone proteins that can drive oncogenic phenotypes, also known as oncohistones [6, 7]. There are several mechanisms whereby oncohistones are thought to promote oncogenesis. Namely, histone mutations may locally perturb the reading, writing, and erasing of histone PTMs; disrupt histone–histone and histone–DNA interactions that are critical for forming stable nucleosomes; impact interfaces that are recognized by chromatin-binding factors; or affect histone tail dynamics [4, 5, 8, 9]. These local perturbations may scale up to global changes in chromatin dynamics, affecting DNA accessibility, repair, and replication, as well as transcription [7]. Growing evidence suggests that histone mutations have a larger impact in cancer than previously thought [6, 10–12]. While some mutations may be inconsequential (i.e. passenger mutations), others have been demonstrated to promote oncogenic phenotypes (i.e. driver mutations). This has led to several histone genes being recognized as cancer driver genes by the Catalogue of Somatic Mutations in Cancer Cancer Gene Census [13]. A handful of mutations (most notably, those in H1 and H3 histones) have been shown to promote cancer development [7, 14] and associate with clinically relevant outcomes such as overall survival [15], tissue invasion [16, 17], and therapeutic response [18]. In recognition of the significance on clinical prognosis, the World Health Organization has expanded its classification of high-grade gliomas to include the H3.3 K27M mutation as a clinical biomarker of highly aggressive tumors [19, 20]. Histones are unique in that they are encoded by multiple genes [21]. Replication-dependent histone genes are found in histone gene clusters, are expressed in a DNA replicationdependent manner (primarily during the S phase), lack introns, and undergo unique 3 -end processing during transcription [22–24]. In contrast, replication-independent histones are not found in gene clusters, their mRNA products can be expressed at any point in the cell cycle, their transcripts are polyadenylated, and they may contain introns, in some cases producing multiple transcripts and different protein isoforms. While a handful of histone mutations have a demonstrated role in oncogenesis, there are many more observed in cancer patients with undetermined consequences [6, 18, 25]. Specifically, it has yet to be determined if these mutations meaningfully contribute to cancer development. Furthermore, mechanisms that allow oncohistone mutations to drive cancer development also remain obscure. To elucidate the role that histone

mutations play in cancer en masse, we have gathered a large whole-exome sequencing (WES) dataset detailing somatic mutations for 11 950 cancer patients among 68 cancer types. Using various computational methods, we identified histone missense mutations that act as probable cancer drivers within specific cancer types. Furthermore, we leveraged histone interaction networks to determine if missense mutations preferentially localize to specific types of histone interaction interfaces. To complement this analysis, we examined if histone missense mutations are likely to disrupt protein–protein and protein–DNA interactions using multiple quantitative methods (SAAMBE-3D, SAMPDI-3D, and MutaBind2) [26–28]. As disruption of histone PTM signaling is an emerging oncogenic mechanism, we also analyzed whether mutations may affect histone PTM sites. Finally, we investigated how histone mutations may drive mutagenesis, given previous studies showing that histone mutations interfere with DNA repair [29, 30]. We found that the frequency of patients with predicted histone driver mutations depends on the cancer type, with malignant diffuse large B-cell lymphoma showing the largest frequency of patients with predicted histone driver mutations. Furthermore, we reported that histone mutations were strongly enriched at acidic patch residues, functionally important sites widely used for histone interactions with protein binding partners. In accordance with this, we found that histone mutations preferentially aggregated at histone interfaces for specific proteins. Our work highlights Tonsoku-Like Protein (TONSL), Death Domain Associated Protein 6 (DAXX), FAcilitates Chromatin Transcription (FACT) complex subunit SSRP1, Histone Chaperone ASF1A, DNA Replication Licensing Factor MCM2, BRCA-1 Associated RING Domain Protein 1 (BARD1), Lysine-Specific Histone Demethylase 1A (LSD1), and Histone acetyltransferase KAT6B as potential mediators of histone mutation-driven oncogenesis. Moreover, and consistent with the role of many of these factors in preserving genome integrity, we noted that patients harboring histone mutations on interfaces with these proteins had a significantly higher tumor mutational burden (TMB) than patients without histone mutations. Of particular interest, we identified several H4 histone mutations that could serve as probable drivers of oncogenesis, further noting that they may affect interactions with the aforementioned binding partners that may act as possible oncogenic mediators. For example, among these, the H4C3 R67P mutation was predicted to be highly disruptive of nucleosome structures and identified as a driver in multiple cancer types. In summary, our findings suggest that histone mutations may drive oncogenesis in a cancer-specific manner by perturbing interactions with other proteins, including DNA repair factors, which, in turn, can lead to high tumor mutation rates.

Identification of histone coding genes To identify all human histone somatic mutations in the WES dataset, a comprehensive list of histone genes was compiled by aggregating publicly available data from the Human Genome Organization Gene Nomenclature Committee [21] and Histone Database 2.0 [32]. In total, the list of histone genes includes 96 distinct histone genes, which encode 59 distinct proteins (Supplementary Table S1). Within the list of histone proteins, and throughout the analysis, only full-length proteins that are encoded by the entirety of the protein’s respective coding sequence were considered. Truncated proteins resulting from alternative splicing were not considered. Although some histone genes are tissue-specific, the vast majority of replication-dependent and replication-independent genes are expressed in human normal tissues [33]. For example, from the Human Protein Atlas, which includes gene expression data from 44 normal human tissue types, 93% of histone genes were expressed in at least one tissue [33]. Based on massspectroscopy data, 91% of histone proteins were detected, confirming the existence of their protein form [34].

Mutation driver status prediction To predict the driver status of missense mutations in histone genes, we used the “rank” module of the MutaGene standalone Python package (https://github.com/PanchenkoLab/mutagene) [35]. MutaGene creates background mutational models (profiles) by aggregating mutation counts for all 96 possible trinucleotide context-dependent point mutations across patients within a given cancer type [36]. To rank mutations by driver status, MutaGene normalizes the observed frequency of the mutation in a given cancer type by an expected rate/frequency derived from the cancer type’s mutational profile. In the case of missense mutations, it considers a penta-nucleotide context around the mutated site. MutaGene reports a B-score, which is the probability of observing a particular mutation in a specific genomic location higher than the observed frequency, given the background mutational rate. The lower the B-score produced by MutaGene, the more likely the mutation is to be ranked as a driver. Therefore, MutaGene predictions rely on the following information: the number of times a given mutation is observed, the background mutational profile, and the size of the patient cohort. Following mutation ranking, the default MutaGene ranking thresholds were applied to classify mutations as “drivers”, “potential drivers”, or “passengers”. As calculations are performed within the scope of a given cancer type, these labels only apply

to the individual cancer type and are not transferable across cancer types. Moreover, because mutation rankings are performed at the gene level, driver status labels do not apply to other genes that encode the same protein, as they may vary in their nucleotide sequence. For stringency, only mutations predicted as “drivers” were included in our analyses, whereas “potential drivers” were not. We note that B-scores were previously shown to improve the classification of cancer drivers by up to 20% compared to the observed mutational frequency alone [35]. When performing MutaGene ranking, patients with extremely high TMBs were removed. TMBs were calculated by dividing the total number of mutations in each sample by 30 Mbp, the size of the human exome. Since TMB varies by cancer type, we calculated TMB Z-scores for each patient based on the TMB distribution in the relevant cancer type and calculated modified Z-scores for cancer types where TMBs were not normally distributed (Supplementary Note 1). Patients with an absolute Z-score > 3 or an absolute modified Z-score > 3.5 were considered outliers and removed before MutaGene mutational background profile creation. Outliers were identified within each cancer type, and only cancer types with at least 10 patients were retained for cancer driver mutation status prediction, including the creation of background mutational profiles.

Annotation of empirical histone interaction interfaces and PTMs Empirical histone interactions were extracted from the structural [provided by atomic resolution X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), and cryo-electron microscopy (Cryo-EM) PDB structures] and chemical crosslinking data of our previously reported human histone interactome [37, 38]. For PDB structures, an interaction was considered if at least one heavy atom (non-hydrogen) of a histone was within 5 Å of a heavy atom of a binding partner [37]. In the chemical cross-linking data, lysine residues which formed lysine cross-links were considered to form interaction interfaces [37, 39]. Interactome data were partitioned into the following types of interaction: histone–histone, histone–DNA, and histone–nonhistone protein (NHP) interactions. Binding partners in the NHP category include proteins that are not histones. A total of 54 histone proteins and 435 binding partners (including DNA, histones, and NHPs) are represented in the interactome. Histone PTMs were gathered from previous data [40]. The list of histone PTMs (Supplementary Table S2) includes PTMs found on most histones within a histone type, as well as PTMs that are unique to individual histone proteins. In total, 818 PTM sites and 20 different chemical modifications were considered across 59 histone proteins. Histone interaction interface, PTMs, and missense mutations were mapped to full-length histone sequences from UniProt [41].

Inference of interfaces using multiple sequence alignments Given that some human histone proteins are not adequately represented in the interactome, empirically derived interactions were supplemented with inferred interactions based on multiple sequence alignments. Namely, multiple sequence alignments were constructed for human histone proteins

third edition of the International Classification of Diseases for Oncology and can be derived from the clinical data provided by the GDC. WES data were gathered for the neoplasms of cancer patients in the form of Mutation Annotation Format (MAF) files. All MAF files follow the GDC Ensemble protocol and are readily available for open-access download via the GDC [31]. Patients with multiple MAF files were identified, and a union of all variants was used to create a single patient-specific MAF file. For cancer-type-specific analyses, patient-level WES data were grouped by patient primary diagnosis. For pan-cancer analyses, all patients were grouped together regardless of primary diagnosis. In total, we collected 2803 distinct histone missense mutations spread across 2029 patients.

Testing associations between histone mutation occurences and their location on interfaces and other functionally important sites Two-tailed Fisher’s exact and binomial tests were performed to test the statistical associations between mutation occurrence and localization to functionally important sites: binding interfaces with DNA, other histones, and NHPs; acidic patches from HistoneDB2.0 [32] and a previously published source [43]; and PTMs from a previously published source [40] (Supplementary Notes 2 and 3). To test associations with interaction interfaces of a specific type (histone– histone, histone–DNA, or histone–NHP), tests were conducted within the context of an individual histone type (H1, H2A, H2B, H3, H4) and further stratified into three groups: all mutations, mutations observed in more than one patient (recurrent mutations), and mutations restricted to a single patient (nonrecurrent mutations). Mutation recurrence was defined at the protein level, that is mutations leading to the same amino acid substitution but affecting different genes that code for the same histone protein were deemed recurrent (Supplementary Note 4). As a measure of effect size, odds ratios (ORs) were calculated in the case of Fisher’s exact tests, whereas fold-changes were calculated for binomial tests (Supplementary Notes 2 and 3). For all tests, Benjamini–Hochberg multiple testing correction with a false discovery rate (FDR) of 0.05 was applied. Adjusted P-values (q-values) <0.05 were considered significant. To determine if histone mutation occurences were associated with PTM sites, tests were conducted within individual histone types. To examine associations between histone mutations and acidic patch residues, tests considered H2A and H2B proteins collectively, as acidic patch residues occur on both of these histone types. Tests for association at acidic patch residues were performed by comparing acidic patch residues to all other H2A/H2B residues or to H2A/H2B residues involved in any binding interaction (not including acidic patch residues). Fisher’s exact and binomial tests were also conducted to determine if histone mutations were associated with interaction interfaces for individual NHPs. Here, all histone proteins were examined collectively. Tests were performed within individual cancer types, in addition to pan-cancer, and were only performed if there were at least ten histone mutations available. Multiple testing corrections, significance thresholds and effect sizes were calculated as previously described. This procedure was repeated when only considering cancer-specific driver mutations.

Predicting the impact of histone mutations on physical interactions SAAMBE-3D was used to predict the effects of mutations on histone–protein binding affinity [26] and SAMPDI3D on histone–DNA interactions [27] (G, measured in kcal/mol). These machine-learning methods use various features (side chain volume, hydrophobicity, hydrogen bond donor/acceptor, polarity, mutation type, chemical nature of the amino acid residue, and others) and are trained on experimental sets of binding affinity changes upon mutations. MutaBind2 was used to verify predicted changes in binding affinity for a selection of interactions, given that this rigorous method requires structure minimization and calculation of interactions with the solvent and is therefore more time-consuming (Supplementary Table S3) [28]. All methods require experimental structures with atomic resolution to predict G. As a result, interactions based exclusively on cross-linking mass spectrometry data could not be examined. SAAMBE-3D and SAMPDI-3D were chosen as the primary methods to predict binding changes, given that they perform relatively well and are very fast in comparison to other methods.

Identifying binding partners that have interfaces disproportionately impacted by histone mutations For each protein binding partner, a Mann–Whitney U test was performed to compare the predicted G value distributions of histone mutations at interfaces for that partner to the predicted G distributions of mutations at interfaces for all other partners. Rank-biserial correlation coefficients were calculated alongside each Mann–Whitney U test. As all available structures were used to obtain G estimates for a given mutation-partner pair, the mean G estimate across all structures was used for this analysis. Tests were only conducted if there were at least 10 unique mutations with a predicted G value available for interactions with a given protein binding partner and at least 10 mutations with a predicted G value available for interactions with other protein binding partners. Tests were performed for pan-cancer and each cancer type.

Comparisons of TMB distributions Kruskal–Wallis tests (to extend Mann–Whitney U test for more than two groups) were conducted to determine if there was a significant difference between TMB distributions of patients without histone mutations, patients with histone mutations not at NHP interfaces, and patients with histone mutations at NHP interfaces. This was done for pan-cancer and individual cancer types. Only cancer types with at least ten patients in each of the groups above were examined. Two kinds of Kruskal–Wallis tests were used (see Supplementary Note 5). Post-hoc Dunn’s tests were run to determine pairwise differences between groups in instances where both Kruskal–Wallis tests reported a significant difference between the above mentioned groups (q < 0.05, Benjamini–Hochberg, FDR = 0.05). Dunn’s test is a nonparametric pairwise multiple comparisons procedure based on rank sums and is a relatively conservative post-hoc test for Kruskal–Wallis. A rank-biserial correlation coefficient was calculated to accompany each pairwise comparison. This analysis was repeated after removing patients with POLE mutations. within the same histone type using Expresso [42]. Alignments included histone tails, H1, and full macroH2A histones. Empirically derived interaction interfaces observed in individual histone proteins were mapped to aligned positions. These interaction interfaces were then inferred across all histone proteins at the aligned positions that did not have a gap. Positions in the alignment where >30% of the aligned proteins showed a gap were not considered in this interface inference process. Inferred interfaces were only used to determine if histone mutations were statistically associated with DNA, histone, or NHP interfaces en masse. Inferred interfaces were not used to determine if histone mutations were associated with interfaces for individual NHPs.

Results Histone missense mutations drive oncogenesis in a cancer-specific manner Tumor WES and clinical data were gathered from the GDC for 11 950 patients. Patients were primarily adults over the age of 18 at the time of diagnosis, except for one individual. Among these patients, missense mutations represented the largest proportion (60%) of all observed histone mutation types, with 2803 distinct histone missense mutations spread across 2029 patients (∼16% of all patients). Given their prevalence, we focused on histone missense mutations and refer to them as “histone mutations” throughout the text. We compared our dataset to an existing data set of cancer histone mutations derived from WES and targeted sequencing studies [6]. After removing targeted sequencing studies from the aforementioned dataset, we found that the overall number of mutations in the data sets were comparable, although 39% of our patients with histone mutations and 38% of histone mutations (1065 mutations) were novel and not present in this previous data set [6]. Unfortunately, we were not able to combine these two data sets because they used different reference genome versions, whereas MutaGene, the tool we used to predict driver mutation status, operates on the gene level and considers the specific genomic context of mutations. Importantly, all H1 histone mutations were novel, as the previous study did not analyze this type of histone family. Histone mutations from our dataset are visualized on aligned replication-dependent histone protein sequences in Supplementary Figs S1–S5. To determine novel drivers of oncogenesis, we applied two complementary and independent approaches: we predicted cancer driver status using MutaGene [35, 36] and examined the association of mutations with sites important for histone functions (e.g. interaction interfaces, PTM sites, and acidic patches), thereby revealing mechanisms by which they may contribute to oncogenesis. MutaGene builds the DNA context-dependent mutational profiles and uses these as background somatic mutational models. Then, MutaGene calculates the probability of observing a particular type of mutation at a given site more frequently (by chance) than the actual number of observed mutations in a given cohort of patients and labels mutations as oncogenic “drivers”, “potential drivers”, or benign “passengers”, based on the calculated probability. Ultimately, the classification of mutations depends on the combined effects of multiple parameters, in-

cluding mutation frequency, background mutability, and cohort size.Because MutaGene predicts driver status using the observed mutation frequency, among others, we removed samples with high TMB for MutaGene driver prediction (see the ‘Materials and Methods’ section). Driver mutation status prediction was conducted in a cancer type-specific manner, by separating mutations based on patient primary diagnosis (68 in total; see the ‘Materials and Methods’ section). It is worth noting that we retained samples with high TMB for other analyses which analyze the TMB variability across different categories (histone-protein interaction and binding affinity). Performing the MutaGene analysis by grouping patients based on cancer type revealed 218 distinct cancer-type specific predicted histone driver mutations across 55 cancer types (Supplementary Table S4). Although most predicted cancer drivers were rare mutations detected in a single patient (189 rare mutations), 29 MutaGene-predicted driver mutations were recurrent and observed in more than one patient. Nevertheless, we note that MutaGene predicts driver mutations on the gene level (to take into account the genomic background mutation rate), and even though a mutation may be rare in one histone gene, it can be recurrent when all genes coding for the same histone protein are considered (see Supplementary Figs S1–S5). Interestingly, we found no significant difference in the number of driver mutations per gene when histone proteins encoded by multiple genes were compared to histone proteins encoded by a single gene (Mann– Whitney U, P = 0.40). Below, we describe analyses that investigate histone mutations at the protein level to further evaluate the plausibility of a histone mutation acting as an oncogenic driver. Six mutations were predicted as drivers in multiple cancer types (Supplementary Table S4). Validating our approach, three of the six mutations, H3 K27M, G34R, and K36M, are well-known cancer driver mutations [7, 10, 11, 44, 45]. Another H2BC21 F70L mutation was reported previously [46], shown to increase H2A/H2B dimer exchange via Nap1 [47], but whether it drives cancer is unknown. We predicted that the H2BC21 F70L mutation has a destabilizing effect on the nucleosome (mean SAAMBE-3D G = 1.53 kcal/mol, mean MutaBind2 G = 1.35 kcal/mol) [26, 28], findings that may be related to its effects on dimer exchange described above. One of another novel drivers, the H3C2 E133Q mutation, is located on H3–H2A and H3–H4 interfaces, with a trend towards destabilizing histone–histone interactions (mean SAAMBE-3D G = 0.80 kcal/mol, mean MutaBind2 G = 1.12 kcal/mol). Importantly, we also predicted a driver mutation in the H4 histone type for the first time, H4C3 R67P. The H4C3 R67P mutation was found to be highly disruptive of nucleosomal histone–histone interactions (mean SAAMBE-3D G = 2.64 kcal/mol; mean MutaBind2 G = 2.14 kcal/mol) [26, 28]. We note that this mutation was also located at histone interfaces with DAXX, MCM2, and BARD1, suggesting that it may disrupt chromatin function by altering the interaction with these proteins. As will be discussed in the next sections, MutaGenepredicted histone driver mutations are found to be significantly enriched in terms of their occurences at interaction interfaces for these partners. Moreover, patients with mutations at interfaces for these partners exhibit high TMB (discussed later). For each NHP group, Mann–Whitney U tests were conducted to determine if the TMB distributions of patients in the NHP group differed significantly from one of the following: the TMB distribution of patients without histone mutations, the TMB distribution of patients with histone mutations not on NHP interfaces, and the TMB distribution of patients with histone mutations at interfaces for other NHPs. Importantly, we found that most mutations were predicted as drivers in a single cancer type, highlighting the cancer-specific nature of histone driver mutations. Namely, when comparing across cancer types, we found that adult glioblastoma (pediatric cancers were not represented in this study), infiltrating duct carcinoma, and papillary carcinoma were significantly depleted [Fisher’s exact, q < 0.05, OR < 1] of all and MutaGene-predicted driver mutations, compared to all other cancer types. In contrast, diffuse large B-cell malignant lymphoma and transitional cell carcinoma

(Fig. 1A) were significantly enriched (OR > 1) with all mutations and MutaGene-predicted driver mutations. Diffuse large B-cell malignant lymphoma showed the strongest enrichment for cancer mutations and MutaGene-predicted drivers (OR for MutaGene-predicted drivers = 43.3 and OR for all mutations = 7.98), where 53% of patients harbored at least one MutaGene-predicted histone driver mutation. Within this cancer type, MutaGene-predicted driver mutations were found across all histone types/families (H3, H4, H2A, H2B, and H1) and were most abundant among the H1 histone type (Fig. 1B). MutaGene-predicted drivers in diffuse large B-cell malignant lymphoma occurred most frequently on H4, H1.4, and H1.2 histone proteins (Fig. 1C).

Histone mutations localize to specific types of interaction interfaces, acidic patch residues, and PTM sites

nificantly enriched only on inferred H4–NHP interfaces. Collectively, our findings suggest that mutations are preferably located on specific types of interfaces when compared to noninterfacial sites, given that the sizes of interfacial and noninterfacial regions are explicitly taken into account by the binomial and Fisher’s exact tests performed. Strong significant enrichments were also found for both recurrent and nonrecurrent mutations on acidic patch residues, relative to non-acidic patch residues (Fig. 2B and Supplementary Fig. S7B). Importantly, a significant enrichment of recurrent mutations on acidic patch residues was also detected when compared against non-acidic residues that are involved in interactions (Supplementary Figs S7B and S8). As for PTM sites, significant enrichments were found with recurrent mutations in H1 and H2A histones, and nonrecurrent mutations in H3 histones, although with a weak effect size. Conversely, H2B recurrent mutations were significantly depleted on PTM sites, relative to non-PTM sites. Results concerning all mutations, regardless of recurrence status, can be seen in Supplementary Fig. S9 and Supplementary Tables S7– S11. To examine associations between histone mutations and interaction interfaces of NHP binding, we examined the localization of histone mutations to interfaces for each of the 366 NHP binding partners captured in the interactome database. Empirical interaction interfaces derived from all available structures and cross-linking mass spectrometry data were considered in these tests. For 133 partners, histone mutation occurences were enriched at binding interfaces with the relevant NHP when compared to residues that are not involved in interactions with the same NHP (binomial and Fisher’s exact test q < 0.05; Fig. 2C). To determine if MutaGene-predicted driver histone mutations associate with interfaces for specific NHPs in specific cancer types, we repeated this analysis by examining each cancer type separately (Supplementary Table S12) and using only MutaGene-predicted driver mutations (Supplementary Table S13). We found that MutaGenepredicted driver mutations were enriched at interfaces with 53 different NHPs in 11 cancer types. Strikingly, for 24 out of these 53 NHPs, the MutaGene-predicted driver mutations occurred within H4 histones (Supplementary Table S14). NHPs that may be disproportionally affected by histone mutations include DAXX, MCM2, ASF1A, TONSL, SSRP1, and BARD1. Focusing on large B-cell malignant lymphoma, the cancer type with the highest frequency of predicted driver mutations, we found that predicted driver mutations were enriched on histone interfaces with TONSL, DAXX, MCM2, TP53-Binding Protein 1 (TP53BP1), and Centromere Protein N (CENPN) (Fig. 2D).

Effects of histone mutations on binding affinity Prompted by our finding that histone mutations significantly associate with specific binding interfaces, we examined if histone mutations lead to quantitative changes in binding affinity (estimated as a difference in binding affinities when comparing wild-type and mutant residues, G). To systematically explore this possibility, we used SAAMBE-3D and SAMPDI-3D to estimate the effects of mutations on histone– protein (Supplementary Table S15) and histone–DNA interactions (Supplementary Table S16), respectively. MutaBind2 was used to verify selected predictions. In total, we obtained

A previous study found that histone mutations co-localize with histone interaction interfaces [6], but whether mutations preferentially occur on these sites of functional importance on histone proteins has not been examined systematically. Recently, we constructed a human histone interactome [37], a comprehensive catalog of histone interaction interfaces at amino acid level resolution constructed from structural (atomic resolution X-ray crystallography, NMR, and cryo-EM PDB structures) and cross-linking mass spectrometry data. Powered by this interactome we asked if the occurrence of histone mutations is significantly associated with histone–histone and histone–DNA interfaces in the nucleosomal context (based exclusively on evidence from nucleosome and chromatosome structures), as well as histone–NHP interfaces (based on all available structures and cross-linking mass spectrometry data). We also examined if histone mutations preferentially localize to acidic patch residues [32, 43] or PTM sites [40], which play important roles in histone and nucleosome function (see the ‘Materials and methods’ section for definitions). Since the interactome, PTM, and acidic patch data are not cancer-type specific, we analyzed all mutations collectively and categorized them by whether they were recurrent (occurring in more than one patient) or nonrecurrent, rather than using the cancer-specific MutaGene driver prediction labels. As the interactome database captures information for specific histone proteins (e.g. H3.1 and H3.3), rather than general histone types (e.g. H3 histones), we tested the association between histone mutations and interaction interfaces based on the existing experimentally derived data as well as after inferring interaction interfaces on other members of the same histone family using protein sequence alignments (see the ‘Materials and methods’ section; Supplementary Fig. S6). Since each statistical test has its own limitations, we performed different statistical tests (binomial and Fisher’s exact) for both types of data supporting the interaction (empirically derived or inferred) (Fig. 2A and B, Supplementary Figs S7–S9, and Supplementary Tables S7–S11). Notably, we found that recurrent and nonrecurrent H2A mutations were significantly enriched on nucleosomal histone–DNA and histone–NHP interfaces when compared to mutations occurring on residues not engaged in these types of interactions, respectively (Fig. 2A and Supplementary Fig. S7A). For H2B histones, recurrent mutations were significantly enriched on nucleosomal histone–histone interfaces when compared to mutations not on nucleosomal histone– histone interfaces. Both recurrent and nonrecurrent H3 mutations were significantly enriched on empirically derived NHP interfaces, but not on inferred interactions. Although the limited representation of H1 proteins in the nucleosomal interactome prevented us from testing empirical H1–DNA interfaces, we did find that recurrent H1 mutations were significantly enriched at inferred H1–DNA interfaces. Having identified significant associations between the occurence of histone mutations and interfaces with histonebinding proteins (Fig. 2), we next examined if mutations are predicted to preferentially affect specific histone–protein binding complexes. To capture the full range of histone interactions, including histone dimer-chaperone interactions, we considered both nucleosomal and non-nucleosomal structures in this analysis. We compared the distributions of changes in binding affinity (G) for a given protein binding partner (histone or NHP) to the distribution of binding affinity changes of all other protein partners. We identified 32 specific binding partners predicted to be disproportionally affected by cancer histone mutations based on G (Mann– Whitney U test, q < 0.05) (Supplementary Table S17). Notably, mutations on interfaces with ASF1A, TONSL, and the heterodimeric FACT complex [48] subunits SSRP1 and SPT16 were found to be significantly more destabilizing (higher G values) when compared to mutations on interfaces for other proteins. When looking at mutations observed within specific cancer types we found that binding with TONSL (adenocarcinoma, endometrioid adenocarcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma), Histone H2B Type 1-J (adenocarcinoma), Histone H2B type 1-C/E/F/G/I (squamous cell carcinoma and multiple myeloma), Chromobox Protein Homolog 1 (squamous cell carcinoma), Peregrin (squamous cell carcinoma), and SPT16 (transitional cell carcinoma) was disproportionately disrupted by histone mutations.

Histone mutations on specific binding interfaces significantly associated with high TMB Finding that histone mutations preferentially localize to interaction interfaces with proteins involved in maintaining genome integrity (e.g. TONSL, DAXX, and ASF1A) prompted us to examine if there is a link between histone mutations and TMB. Kruskal–Wallis and post-hoc Dunn’s tests showed that patients with histone mutations (both interfacial and nonin-

terfacial) had significantly higher TMB compared to patients without histone mutations in pan-cancer (Fig. 4A) and in several cancer types (q < 0.05, rank-biserial correlation coefficient ranges from 0.16 to 0.86; Supplementary Tables S18 and S19). Furthermore, significantly higher TMB was found in patients with interfacial histone mutations when compared to patients with histone mutations not on NHP interfaces in two cancer types: serous cystadenocarcinoma (rank-biserial correlation coefficient = 0.35) and endometrioid adenocarcinoma (rank-biserial correlation coefficient = 0.28). Finding a link between histone mutations and TMB is consistent with growing evidence that histone mutations can promote mutation accumulation [29, 49, 50]. To investigate if this may involve interactions with specific NHPs, we examined if patients with mutations at specific NHP interfaces have a significantly higher TMB compared to patients without histone mutations, with histone mutations not on NHP interfaces, and with histone mutations at interfaces for other NHPs (Mann–Whitney U, q < 0.05, see the ‘Materials and methods’ section). Examining all cancer patients together identified MCM2, DAXX, LSD1, SSRP1, KAT6B, TONSL, ASF1A, and BARD1 as binding partners that may link histone mutations to high TMB (Fig. 4C–E and Supplementary Table S20). Consistent with this finding, we note that when all cancer patients were considered, histone mutations were significantly enriched at interfaces with all these binding partners with high effect sizes (Supplementary Table S12). Furthermore, histone mutations affecting histone interactions with SSRP1, ASF1A, and TONSL had significantly higher predicted G values when compared to other proteins (Fig. 4B). In contrast, KAT6B had significantly lower predicted G values when compared to other proteins, although these mutations were still destabilizing (G > 0; Fig. 4B). In the context of specific cancer types, histone mutations localizing to interaction interfaces with DAXX, SPT16, MCM2, ASF1A, and TONSL were also found to be associated with high TMB in more than one cancer type (Supplementary Table S20). Similar results were obtained when Mann–Whitney U tests using permutations to generate a null distribution were performed (Supplementary Table S20). We previously showed a positive association between the presence of histone and POLE mutations [25]. Given that mutations in POLE, the gene that encodes DNA polymerase ε, are associated with extremely high TMB [51, 52], we repeated our analyses after removing patients with POLE mutations. We found that the significant differences in TMB between patients with and without histone mutations persisted, indicating that our results were likely not biased by POLE mutant status (rank-biserial correlation coefficient ranges from 0.14 to 0.67; Supplementary Tables S21 and S22, and Supplementary Fig. S10). Furthermore, we found that histone mutations that overlapped TONSL and DAXX interfaces were associated with high TMB, independent of the presence of POLE mutations (Supplementary Table S23).

Discussion Dysregulated epigenetic states are hallmarks of cancer that can arise from genetic alterations in epigenetic regulators, including histones. While a few histone mutations have been documented to drive cancer development, many others have been reported, but their contribution to cancer biology remains to be examined. In this work, we aimed to provide insight into the mechanisms by which histone mutations may generally contribute to oncogenesis as well as how they may do so in specific cancer types. Building from previous studies showing that cancer mutations can promote oncogenesis by disrupting protein–protein interaction networks, PTMs, and other aspects of normal histone function [55], we examined histone mutations en masse using complementary and independent information from genomic, structural, and biophysical data. Although there are prediction methods that show cancer driver mutation status can be predicted using specific functional features [56, 57], it is well known that cancer-specific driver mutations are notoriously difficult to predict, especially rare ones [58]. Moreover, histone mutations in cancer have been shown to induce long-range allosteric effects [49]. Collectively, our work provides a unique view into the mechanisms by which histone mutations may drive oncogenesis, and offers critical insights that can be used to develop novel biomarkers and therapeutics. Our findings indicate that most histone mutations operate in a cancer type-specific manner, aligning closely with

the cell-type specificity inherent to epigenetic regulation. We have identified several adult cancer types with disproportionately high levels of histone mutations, suggesting that these genetic alterations may play a relevant oncogenic role. Specifically, diffuse large B-cell malignant lymphoma stands out, with 62% of patients harboring at least one histone mutation and over half harboring a predicted driver histone mutation. Conversely, several cancer types, including glioblastoma, were depleted for histone mutations compared to all other cancer types, again emphasizing the cancer-specific nature of histone mutations. We note that even though histone mutations have been reported to be remarkably frequent in pediatric glioblastoma cases [10, 11], the presence of these mutations is rare among adults [11].

Second, as discussed above, previous work, including our own, has shown that histone mutations can directly cause mutation accumulation [29, 30, 49]. A key challenge of understanding the mechanisms by which histone mutations drive oncogenesis is the fact that replication-dependent histone genes are encoded by large gene families. Given that most oncohistones described to date disrupt function in a dominant manner [7, 10, 11, 44, 45] suggests that in order to drive cancer, mutations must overcome the assumed redundancy of histone genes. Nevertheless, several lines of evidence suggest that histone genes may not be completely redundant, providing an explanation of how mutations in a histone gene may contribute to oncogenesis despite the presence of wild-type copies in the cell. First, there is evidence that some histone genes are expressed at higher levels than others in cancer, making them disproportional contributors to the total histone pool in the cell [71]. Second, growing evidence suggests that genes encoding the same type of histone are not entirely functionally redundant [72]. Third, it is possible that loss-of-function mutations on histone genes ultimately low the functional pool of histones in the cell, and this ultimately disrupts function. Consistently, decreasing the total pools of histone H4 in budding yeast leads to the activation of genes that are normally repressed [73], a phenotype that has been reported for a handful of cancer histone mutations [74, 75]. In summary, using a computational framework that integrates genomic and proteomic data allowed us to identify histone mutations and several predominant mechanisms by which they may drive oncogenesis. We propose that perturbing genomic integrity may be a common mechanism of oncogenesis for histone mutations. We demonstrated that these mutations can disrupt interactions with several histonebinding partners, each having distinct roles in cellular function. Although our work and that of others indicate that individual histone mutations may be rare, we show that analyzing them based on common structural and functional features is a powerful strategy to illuminate their relationship to cancer. As the presence of cancer mutations may be associated with patient prognosis, determining if, and how, histone mutations are likely to contribute to cancer development is critical to developing biomarkers or therapeutic interventions [15, 76–78].

Nucleosomes function as key hubs in epigenetic signaling pathways, with histone proteins playing central roles in facilitating interactions with DNA, other histones, and NHPs. Based on this, we investigated whether chromatin destabilization at the nucleosomal level and the disruption of interactions with NHPs may be predominant effects of histone mutations. While mutations in specific histone types were statistically associated with PTM sites, histone–histone, and histone–DNA interactions, one of the strongest significant enrichments for histone mutations was observed at acidic patch residues, as was suggested previously [6]. Given that the acidic patch is a region that facilitates interactions between nucleosomes and NHPs [59, 60] and that histone mutations were significantly enriched at interfaces with specific NHPs, histone mutations may drive cancer development through major mechanisms other than direct disruption of nucleosome structures or PTM sites. Instead, these mutations may interfere with the function or recruitment of specific histone-binding partners. A notable example of this is H4 cancer-specific driver mutations, which we found to disproportionately accumulate on binding interfaces with 24 different NHPs, even though. This finding was surprising, given that H4 is a highly conserved histone with no previously documented driver mutations. A key finding from our study is that a common mechanism by which histone mutations may contribute to oncogenesis is by disrupting interactions with key proteins involved in genome integrity. These findings are in line with previous work showing that the H3 G34R, H2B G52D, and H2B P102L mutations dysregulate interactions with proteins involved in mismatch repair and homologous recombination, with cells harboring mutations exhibiting genomic instability [29, 30, 50]. To determine if histone mutations are implicated in genome integrity and associated with high TMB, we merged statistical approaches to examine enrichment/depletion of mutations with respect to specific binding interfaces, measures of quantitative changes in binding affinity produced by mutations, and the TMB status of cancer patients. This led us to identify ASF1A, BARD1, DAXX, KAT6B, LSD1, MCM2, SSRP1, and TONSL as histone-binding partners predicted to be disproportionally affected by cancer histone mutations, possibly acting as conduits for these mutations to increase TMB. DAXX plays several roles in the response to DNA damage, promoting p53 activation [61], regulating RAD51 expression [62], and depositing H3.3 to newly synthesized DNA after DNA damage [63]. ASF1A, MCM2, and TONSL are recruited to H3–H4 dimers near double-stranded breaks to initiate homologous recombination by recruiting RAD51 [53, 64–66]. Furthermore, TONSL functions with the FACT complex and BRCA1/BARD1 in the repair of ultraviolet-induced DNA damage [67, 68]. LSD1, an H3 K4me2 and H4 K20 demethylase [69, 70], is also recruited to sites of DNA damage, with LSD1 loss leading to γ-irradiation hypersensitivity [69]. Given that the absence of methylation on H4 K20 is critical for TONSL recruitment to DNA damage sites [53], is it possible that histone mutations may ultimately disrupt the same or similar mechanisms in different ways. As such, our work generates several interesting hypotheses that warrant detailed experimental examination. While it is formally possible that histone mutations may be byproducts of high TMB in cancer patients, several lines of evidence suggest that they may actually be contributors to significantly elevated TMB levels observed in cancer patients.
